title: The Next US Presidential Election Will Be About AI | NOEMA
gist: Gardels argues the 2028 US race will be the first 'AI election', fought over how to regulate frontier models and how to distribute AI-generated wealth. A bipartisan convergence is forming around giving the public an equity stake in AI companies (sovereign wealth fund / 'universal basic capital') as AI decouples productivity and wealth from labor income.

insights.claim: AI is decoupling productivity growth and wealth creation from jobs and income, with recent productivity gains accruing as corporate profits while labor income falls as a share of US GDP (per Bank of America Institute analysts).
insights.speaker: Nathan Gardels
insights.so_what: If labor's GDP share keeps falling, equity-owners capture AI gains; watch corporate margin expansion vs. wage growth as the structural trade behind the policy fight.
insights.quote: AI innovations are increasingly decoupling productivity growth and wealth creation from jobs and income.

insights.claim: The value created by AI is flowing mostly to tech titans who 'own the robots' and to the top 10% who own 93% of all equities, while labor's bargaining power rapidly diminishes.
insights.speaker: Nathan Gardels
insights.so_what: Concentration of equity ownership is the political pressure point that all the redistribution/pre-distribution proposals target.
insights.quote: the value created by intelligent machines is flowing mostly to those tech titans who “own the robots” and to the top 10% who own 93% of all equities

insights.claim: AI wealth-sharing proposals split into two camps: redistribution of income (higher taxes on AI firms/billionaires funding UBI and services) versus pre-distribution of wealth (giving the public direct ownership stakes in the AI economy upfront, termed 'universal basic capital').
insights.speaker: Nathan Gardels
insights.so_what: The framing determines whether future policy taxes AI cash flows or transfers AI equity to public funds — a direct overhang on AI-company cap tables.
insights.quote: These proposals fall into two rough camps: redistribution of income and pre-distribution of wealth.

insights.claim: Elizabeth Warren argues the current tax code penalizes hiring humans (payroll taxes) while subsidizing capital equipment (tax breaks), effectively incentivizing firms to replace workers with AI, and proposes raising corporate/capital-gains taxes and a data-center excise tax.
insights.speaker: Nathan Gardels
insights.so_what: A data-center excise tax would hit the AI buildout's cost structure directly; watch for it as the most actionable near-term legislative lever.
insights.quote: companies pay payroll taxes for their workers but get tax breaks for investing in technology—effectively, a tax penalty for hiring human beings and a tax break for buying equipment.

insights.claim: Bernie Sanders is drafting legislation for an AI Sovereign Wealth Fund funded by a one-time 50% tax paid in company stock (not profits) from OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI and others, giving the federal government voting shares and board seats to block harmful decisions.
insights.speaker: Nathan Gardels
insights.so_what: A 50%-equity government take would massively dilute AI-company shareholders; even a diluted version reprices private AI valuations and governance.
insights.quote: through a one-time 50 percent tax — not on the profits of OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI and other companies, but paid with something far more valuable than that: the stock

insights.claim: OpenAI's Sam Altman proposed seeding a public wealth fund with a 2.5% tax on companies above a market-cap threshold, payable via equity transfer, and personally lobbied Sanders that a 50% take was too extreme while agreeing with the concept.
insights.speaker: Nathan Gardels
insights.so_what: Incumbent AI CEOs endorsing equity-based public funds signals the principle is near-consensus; the live debate is the rate, which sets the dilution magnitude.
insights.quote: Altman has proposed seeding the fund with a 2.5% tax on companies above a certain market-capitalization threshold, payable through the transfer of equity.

insights.claim: Republicans including Ted Cruz back a public wealth fund but oppose direct government ownership of AI firms, preferring individual public accounts held as index/mutual funds and 'voluntary' equity transfers.
insights.speaker: Nathan Gardels
insights.so_what: Bipartisan agreement on the goal but disagreement on government-vs-individual ownership shapes whether AI equity flows to a state fund or to retail accounts.

insights.claim: Trump publicly mulled the public owning equity shares in AI companies, calling it 'almost a partnership with the American public', and previously floated a national sovereign wealth fund.
insights.speaker: Nathan Gardels
insights.so_what: A sitting Republican president warming to public AI equity stakes broadens the policy's bipartisan runway into 2028.
insights.quote: There is something very interesting about it, where it almost becomes a partnership with the American public.

themes.theme: Existing platforms for universal basic capital

insights.claim: Trump's 'Money Accounts for Growth and Advancement' deposit $1,000 into a personal market-invested account for every US citizen under 8, which Gardels argues could become the foundational platform for broader universal basic capital.
insights.speaker: Nathan Gardels
entities: Money Accounts for Growth and Advancement
insights.so_what: An existing child-investment-account program is the scaffolding pre-distribution advocates would scale up; watch it as the policy vehicle.

insights.claim: California's CalKids program, an embryonic form of universal basic capital championed by Gavin Newsom, deposits up to $1,500 of government money into a professionally-invested account for every low-income first grader, compounding until college age.
insights.speaker: Nathan Gardels
insights.so_what: Newsom (a 2028 Democratic frontrunner) tying UBC to a working state program signals the likely shape of a national pre-distribution pitch.

themes.theme: AI regulation as global public good

insights.claim: AI safety protocols must be treated as a global public good agreed between the US and China, because absent shared restrictions neither superpower can trust its own security and both will race for advantage.
insights.speaker: Nathan Gardels
insights.so_what: Frames AI regulation as an arms-control problem; US-China coordination (or its absence) is a tail risk for frontier-model deployment policy.
insights.quote: This is why AI safety protocols must be considered a global public good that both AI superpowers agree on and adhere to.

insights.claim: Frontier models like Anthropic's 'Mythos' are deemed dangerous for identifying software vulnerabilities and enabling mass cyberattacks, restricting them to a handful of trusted companies and governments, while the Trump White House calls for voluntary pre-release vetting.
insights.speaker: Nathan Gardels
insights.so_what: Voluntary vetting and access-restriction of the most capable models foreshadows a regulatory regime that could gate frontier-model commercialization.
